tokens.text: Campaign
Tutorial
Challenges
Gameplay Basics
Beginner tutorial in which you will learn the basics of playing Heroes of Might and Magic.
Advanced Tutorial
Get familiar with the more advanced mechanics of Heroes of Might and Magic.
Pathfinding
Learn more about the mechanics of terrain penalties.
Marketplaces & Resources
Learn how to exchange between different types of resources using the Marketplace.
Weekly Growth
Learn what affects weekly creature growth in your cities.
Counterattack
Learn combat tactics that minimize losses among your units and lead to victory — even against seemingly stronger opponents.
Spell Levels
Learn how to win battles relying solely on your heroes’ spells.
Areas & Map Spells
Learn how the global map is divided into areas and how these areas affect the use of global map spells and gameplay.
Hero Chaining
Learn how to use multiple heroes effectively to accomplish tasks that would be impossible for a single hero alone.
Flee & Surrender
Learn how to use the options to flee or surrender from battle. These choices can save you in difficult situations.
Main Quest
Defeat the enemy
Complete intermediary tasks
Intermediary Task #1
Capture nearby resource sources
Capture Sawmill
Capture Ore Mine
Capture Gold Mine
Intermediary Task #2
Capture the city to the northwest
Intermediary Task #3
Defeat enemy hero blocking your way
Main Quest
Capture the city in the northeast of the map
Tutorial
Move to the marked area
Follow the road
Start a new game day
Interact with the Redwood Observatory
Collect 5,000 Gold
Attack the enemy further down the road
Recruit reinforcements
Continue on your way
Now that you’ve learned the basics of movement, make your way to the marked checkpoint. You can always view your active tasks in the upper‑left corner of the screen.
Well done on completing the first task! Now, proceed to the next checkpoint along the road — it will help your hero to move faster.
Your hero can no longer move — all Movement points have been used up.
Click the large hourglass button at the bottom right of the screen to start a new day. Your hero will fully restore his Movement points and can continue moving.
Great, your hero’s Movement points have been fully restored. Keep moving to the marked square.

You have discovered an interactive object: the Redwood Observatory — a special structure that removes the fog of war in a large radius around it. Throughout the game, you will encounter many interactive objects. To learn what each one does, simply hover your cursor over them.
Interact with the Redwood Observatory to continue the tutorial. To do this, double-click it with the left mouse button.
You have uncovered a large area on the map, revealing new objects. The passage ahead is guarded by an enemy, and piles of Gold lie scattered nearby.
Before attacking the enemy and moving on, collect all the Gold around you. As you do so, the current amount of each resource will be displayed in the resource panel at the top of the screen.
Keep going and plot a route to the squad blocking your path. Your hero will start a battle as soon as he steps onto the square adjacent to that squad.
Congratulations on winning your first battle!
This time you faced a weak enemy, but on your journey you’ll encounter much stronger foes. To defeat them, you will need a larger army.
Nearby, you can see the Swordsmen’s Garrisons — special buildings where you can hire reinforcements.
Use the Gold you’ve collected to recruit new troops and clear the path ahead.
Continue forward — your strengthened army can now take on a tougher enemy.
Well done! You’ve completed the first part of the tutorial and now know the basics of the game.
In the next tutorial mission, you will learn more advanced features and detailed strategies.
Welcome to the tutorial! Here you will learn the basics of the game. To begin, close this dialog by clicking the left mouse button on the window.
Tutorial
Welcome to the second tutorial mission!
Your goal is to reach the city at the opposite end of the map and capture it.
The city is guarded by an AI player, so prepare your hero and army for the upcoming battle.
Start by capturing the nearby mines — they will provide you with resources and become the basis of your economy.
Congratulations! You have completed the first intermediate task and captured all the nearby mines.
Now build up the city, strengthen your army and play at your own pace.
When you are ready, move along on the road to the main goal.
During your adventures, you will expand your territory, capturing new lands and cities.
The passage to the neighboring area is guarded — win the battle to clear the way.
After that, capture the city located in the new area.
You have successfully captured the city. Complete the exploration of the nearby lands and prepare for a longer trip.
Ahead of you is your first serious opponent — the enemy hero, blocking the way to the next goal.
There are also guards inside some of the objects on the map. Be ready to fight if you want to get the reward hidden inside.
In front of you is an Abandoned Outpost, a special object that will become a new city under your control upon capture.
Defeat the enemy guarding it to gain control of the Outpost.
After that, prepare for the final battle — ahead of you is an enemy hero defending the Necromancers’ city.
Congratulations! You have successfully completed the first tutorial mission and learned the basics of the game.
A more challenging mission is now available, offering a deeper introduction to the mechanics of Heroes of Might and Magic.
Congratulations on completing the basic tutorial! You can replay any mission on the map at any time to refresh your knowledge of the game mechanics.
Main Information
Welcome to the tutorial!
Heroes of Might and Magic: Olden Era is a tactical turn‑based game where you will explore maps, recruit and level up heroes, develop cities, and battle your foes.
Throughout the game, you will encounter various mechanics. These will be introduced and explained step-by-step in this tutorial.
Your journey begins with the Gameplay Basics mission, where you will learn the core elements of the game.
Once completed, you’ll unlock a series of short challenges designed to deepen your understanding of the game’s systems.
To begin the tutorial, use the menu on the right side of the screen.
Main Information
Welcome to the tutorial!
Heroes of Might and Magic: Olden Era is a tactical turn-based game with deep gameplay and a great variety of mechanics that may not be immediately obvious to newcomers.
We’ve prepared several short tutorial challenges to help you learn some of the game’s more intricate mechanics.
To start any of the challenges, use the menu on the right side of the screen.
You can replay any of the challenges whenever you wish.
Game Factions
You currently control one hero — who will be familiar from the previous training mission — and one city. Both belong to the Temple faction.
A game faction is a concept that groups together specific heroes, creatures, and other entities. You can play as different factions, each offering a unique and exciting experience.
Remember: in Heroes of Might and Magic, you play your way! You can hire heroes from different factions, recruit creatures from different factions into your army, and much more.
Global Map
When you enter the game, you will find yourself on the global map — the game world where various objects, locations, and enemy forces are scattered.

By default, most of the map is covered by the fog of war — a visual effect that obscures the landscape and hides objects. To lift the fog, you must move your heroes, revealing new areas and interacting with special objects that uncover more of the world.

To move a hero, double‑click on the map using your left mouse button: the first click will mark your path, and the second will confirm it to send your selected hero on a march. Movement consumes hero’s Movement points.

Movement points are one of a hero’s most important resources. Once depleted, the hero can no longer move across the map and loses access to most actions. However, even with zero Movement points, heroes can still interact with objects on adjacent tiles — such interactions do not require movement and consume no Movement points.

You can only plot a route through areas that are not hidden by fog of war. Obstacles and enemy squads block movement and prevent route planning. Depending on the movement target, the route will be highlighted in different colors: • Green — a regular route the hero can fully travel this turn. • Grey — the hero doesn’t have enough Movement points to reach this part of the path this turn. • Red — movement will end in a battle with an enemy squad or garrison.
Depending on what it is pointing at, the mouse cursor will change to indicate available actions or warn you if movement is impossible.

If the cursor turns gray, the hero cannot reach the targeted spot: they don’t have enough Movement points, the path is blocked by an enemy, terrain, or other interactive objects (e. g. resource pile).

During the game, you will encounter large territories that cannot fit entirely on the screen. At any time, you can move the camera to see the part of the map you need. To do this, use any of the following:
• Hold the right mouse button and drag the cursor. • Use the W, A, S and D keys. • Click on the mini‑map. • Move your mouse cursor to one of the edges of the screen to start panning the map.
Days and Weeks
In Heroes of Might and Magic, the gameplay is divided into days, weeks and months.

To succeed, use your resources wisely each day:
• Move your heroes carefully, keeping in mind their limited Movement points.
• Explore the map and interact with important objects.
• Recruit troops to strengthen your heroes’ armies and unlock new areas.
• Construct new buildings in your cities to develop them.
To develop a city, open its screen in one of the following ways:
• Move a hero to the city’s guest square by plotting a route to it.
• Double‑click the city’s model on the global map.
• Double‑click the city’s icon in the city panel on the right side of the screen.
Remember, each city you control is unique. Buildings constructed in one city will not automatically appear in other cities.
At the start of each new day, several important events take place:
• Heroes’ Movement points are fully restored. • You can construct new buildings in your cities. • Temporary effects may activate or expire, depending on their duration.
Every 7 days, a new week begins, and every 4 weeks marks the start of a new month.
These time intervals also trigger major game events — most notably, creature growth: the number of units available for recruitment increases in cities and in special objects on the map.
To start a new day, click the End Day button in the bottom‑right corner of the screen.
Cities
Cities are the cornerstone of your progress in the game. Each one is unique and offers vital functions that can empower your forces:

Building: Construct new buildings to expand your city’s capabilities. Buildings unlock features such as army recruitment, unit upgrades, trading, and more. Construction requires resources and, at times, specific prerequisite buildings. By default, one building can be built in each city per day.

Recruiting Creatures: One of a city’s core functions is the recruitment of troops. Building creature dwellings allows you to recruit units — their available numbers replenish at the start of each week. Units from previous weeks will accumulate if not recruited.
Upgrading Creatures: Upgraded dwellings allow you to enhance your units. Keep in mind: only creatures whose improved dwellings are present in that city can be upgraded there.
Hiring Heroes: Once a Tavern is built, you can recruit additional heroes. While not essential, having multiple heroes gives you greater flexibility and control over the map.

Trading Resources and Artifacts: Construct a Marketplace to exchange resources and artifacts, helping you manage your economy more efficiently. The more Marketplaces you own, the better rates you’ll get.
Unique Faction Abilities. Every faction offers distinct advantages. Cities tied to a specific faction provide access to exclusive features that can dramatically shift the game in your favor.

Each city can hold up to two heroes at a time: • One in the guest position — this hero is visible in the city and on the global map. • One in the garrison — this hero is stationed in the city and disappears from the global map while there.

You can freely move heroes and creature stacks between the guest position and the garrison during your turn. However, keep the following in mind: • Every hero must have at least one creature stack. A hero without an army is considered defeated. • Apart from neutral squads, creature stacks cannot exist on the map without a hero. You cannot transfer creatures from the garrison to the guest position unless there is a hero present there. • Once transferred by a hero, creature stacks can remain in the garrison without a hero.
Additionally, cities generate Gold daily and accumulate Law points, which are used to activate your faction’s special bonuses.
Base and Upgraded Creatures
When certain conditions are met, your creatures can be upgraded into more powerful ones. Each faction unit has two distinct upgrade paths, offering flexibility in how you build your army.
Upgraded creatures come with improved stats and new abilities. Each upgrade path supports a unique role or playstyle, allowing you to tailor your forces to suit your strategy.

Creature upgrades are performed in cities. There are two ways you can do this: • Use the Upgrade Creatures button • Double‑click a specific stack icon to open its details window and press the Upgrade button there.
Important: You can only upgrade creatures in a city that has the corresponding upgraded dwellings already built.
Beyond the standard upgrade system, there may be alternative upgrade methods, such as special objects on the global map that offer unique transformation options.
Defensive Fortifications
Defensive fortifications are a vital part of your city’s infrastructure. They bolster your defenses by adding battlefield elements such as walls, towers, and other structures during sieges.
Upgrading the fortifications not only strengthens your city’s defensive capabilities but also increases weekly creature growth.

Each time you construct or upgrade fortifications, you’ll be prompted to choose one of three unique improvements. Your selection determines which additional defenses will appear in battle or what new properties will be granted to existing structures. Each choice has its own advantages, so select the one that meets your needs the most!
Passive Income of Cities
Economy plays a central role in Heroes of Might and Magic. Throughout the game, you’ll encounter many sources of income, but cities are among the most important.
Certain buildings within a city generate resources automatically. The primary resource produced daily is Gold, used for construction, recruitment, and other essential actions.
Constructing specific buildings will increase daily Gold income and may also unlock the ability to harvest additional resource types.

Cities also generate: • Law points, a special resource required to activate your faction’s unique bonuses. • Astrology points, a special resource that increases the level of the Magic Observatory and allows you to unlock and upgrade powerful global map spells.

To boost daily Gold, Law and Astrology point production, upgrade your city’s central structure (e.g., the Solar Temple). Note: Cities aren’t the only source of those resources — you can acquire them through other means as you explore the world.
Magic Basics

Mage Guilds are one of the primary sources of spells in Heroes of Might and Magic: Olden Era. Each level of the Guild you build unlocks additional spells in the Magic Observatory, which visiting heroes may be able to learn.
Heroes who begin their day in a city with a Mage Guild also fully restore their mana, allowing them to cast spells again.
To explore spells in more detail and manage your magical capabilities, open the Magic Observatory screen.
Faction Cities
Each faction in Heroes of Might and Magic: Olden Era has its own cities, which offer both universal functions and faction‑specific features.

In all cities, you can (among other things): • Build a Tavern to recruit new heroes. • Use the Marketplace to exchange resources. • Visit the Artifact Merchant to purchase equipment for your heroes using resources.
In addition to these shared options, each faction has unique buildings that are only available in its cities. For example, as the ruler of a Temple city, you can construct two exclusive structures:
• Scouting Skyship, which reveals a wide area of the fog of war around the city.
• Guild of Six Winds, which allows you to train heroes, improving their attributes.
Neutral Squads
Creature Info
On the global map, you will encounter neutral squads — hostile creatures that guard valuable items, important objects, or passageways to new territories.

Neutral squads can feature one or more creatures at the same time. The creature model on the map represents the strongest stack in its army. Hover your cursor over it to see the creature types and suspected amounts of guards you will face.
Also, pay attention to the estimated difficulty of the battle — this indicator shows how challenging the encounter is likely to be for your selected hero.
Both neutral squads and your heroes’ armies can include a wide variety of creatures. Each creature has its own stats, passive traits, and active abilities.

You cannot normally view detailed information about enemy hero creatures until you’re in battle with them. However, you can always check the stats and abilities of creatures in your own army — simply double‑click the portrait of the desired unit in the bottom part of the screen.
Map Objects
As you explore the world, you will come across a wide variety of map objects — buildings and items your hero can interact with.
Hovering over any map object will display a description, helping you decide whether it is currently useful to visit.
Just like with standard movement, you can set a route to a map object. Once confirmed, the hero will automatically move to it and interact upon arrival.
Each object has specific interaction squares, usually located just below it. When setting a destination, your hero’s path will be planned accordingly.
Owned Objects
Some interactive objects on the map have an owner — the player who last interacted with them.
These objects are marked with a flag next to their model on the map. The color of the flag indicates which player currently controls the object.
Ownable objects include cities as well as other key structures, such as:
Mines — These generate resources for your treasury on a daily basis, while flagged. Mines are a cornerstone of your economy.

Creature dwellings — These allow you to hire reinforcements. Like in cities, the number of available units is limited, with a new batch showing up at the start of each week. While flagged, these dwellings passively increase the weekly growth of the corresponding creatures in all your cities.
Resource System
Gold, Wood, Ore, Crystals, Gems, Mercury, and Alchemical Dust are the main resources in the game.

They are essential for nearly all gameplay activities — constructing buildings, hiring troops and heroes, studying and upgrading spells, and more.
Your objective is to gather a variety of resources and use them strategically to strengthen your position over time.
Resources can be obtained in several ways: • Found directly on the global map • Acquired by capturing mines • Collected by visiting objects across the map.
Hero Skills and Levels
Hero Skills
By exploring the world and defeating opponents, your hero earns experience points. Once enough experience is gained, the hero levels up.
With each new level, the hero receives random improvements to their attributes and selects from one of three skills — passive abilities that enhance their overall performance.
Choose your skills wisely to strengthen your hero. Heroes with well-thought-out skill combinations will play a major role in your success.
Each hero has four attributes: • Attack • Defense • Spell Power • Knowledge
Attack and Defense are added to the corresponding stats of creatures in combat, increasing the Damage they deal or reducing the Damage they take.
Spell Power boosts the effectiveness of spells — higher values mean increased spell Damage and longer durations for positive or negative effects.
Knowledge increases the hero’s maximum mana, allowing for more spellcasting during combat.
Each hero also affects two combat stats: • Morale • Luck These are applied to all creatures in the hero’s army.
Morale affects creature behavior: high Morale can give a stack an extra turn, while low Morale can cause it to skip its turn.
Luck influences the chance of landing a Lucky (enhanced) or Unlucky (weakened) Strike.
Important: The greater the number of factions present in your army, the lower its overall Morale. An army composed of one faction gains +1 Morale. Each additional faction reduces Morale by ­–1.
Hero Classes and Skill Progression
The attributes gained at each level depend on the hero’s class:
• Might heroes are more likely to gain Attack and Defense. • Magic heroes are more likely to gain Spell Power and Knowledge.
Similarly, the probability of acquiring specific skills varies:
• Might heroes tend to learn army‑focused skills. • Magic heroes are more likely to be offered spell‑enhancing skills.
Each skill has three tiers: Basic, Advanced, and Expert. As the hero levels up, the skill can be advanced as follows:
• Upon reaching the Advanced tier, you choose one of three subskills.
• Upon reaching the Expert tier, you choose one of three final subskills. Each upgrade improves the core effect of the skill and allows you to tailor your hero to your preferred playstyle.

In addition, your hero can attain an elite class. To do so, you’ll need to acquire a specific combination of maximum level skills. It’s a challenging path, but the reward is worth it — the hero will become significantly stronger or gain powerful new abilities.

To find out which skills are required to unlock an elite class, hover your mouse over the class icon in the hero’s profile. Also, pay attention to the hints shown when levelling up — if a selected skill brings you closer to an elite class, a small icon of the corresponding class will appear next to it.
At any point during the game, you can view detailed information about your heroes.
To do this, double‑click a hero’s portrait in the bottom part of the screen.
Hero Info
You can view all detailed information about your hero in the corresponding interface.
Each hero has four attributes: • Attack • Defense • Spell Power • Knowledge And two combat stats: • Morale • Luck

As your hero levels up, they can learn up to 8 skills, including any skills the hero starts with. Each skill: • Has three possible tiers of proficiency: Basic, Advanced, and Expert. • Can be enhanced with 2 different subskills selected upon reaching Advanced and Expert proficiency.
Some skills can enhance others, and certain skill combinations can unlock an elite hero class, greatly increasing their power and granting access to unique bonuses.
Heroes can equip various artifacts that provide various benefits. Each artifact occupies a specific equipment slot and can only be placed in that slot.
Each hero can equip the following:
• 1 Main‑Hand Weapon and 1 Off‑Hand Weapon • 1 Head artifact, 1 Armor, 1 Belt, 1 pair of Boots, 1 Back artifact • 2 Rings • 4 Items • 1 Holy Relic — a special artifact that enhances the hero’s Skills
Magic
Magic is a key element of gameplay, alongside armies. Heroes can use spells both in combat and on the global map.
To cast spells, heroes use a special resource — mana.
Mana is a limited resource that partially restores each new day. If the hero starts the day in a city that contains a Mage Guild, their mana is fully restored.
Heroes can only use spells that have been added to their Spellbook.
Spells are divided into four schools:
• Daylight Magic • Nightshade Magic • Arcane Magic • Primal Magic
Each school has its own focus — for example: • Daylight Magic includes many support spells that strengthen your army. • Primal Magic mainly contains combat spells that deal Damage.
The main way to acquire spells is by building Mage Guilds in cities.
All spells are ranked by tier, from 1st to 5th.

By default, all heroes can learn and cast 1st- and 2nd-tier spells. To access higher‑tier spells, they must first develop proficiency in the corresponding Magic School, which is acquired like all other skills. Some artifacts may allow heroes to ignore this restriction.

There are also Neutral spells that do not belong to any school. These spells have unique acquisition conditions — most often, they are unlocked through skills and subskills. To use these Neutral spells, a hero must also reach a certain level. The higher the spell’s tier, the higher the required hero level.
Faction Laws

Each faction in the game is unique: its cities feature special buildings, it recruits unique units, and its heroes belong to that specific faction. On the global map, each faction is also associated with its own type of terrain, which affects various game mechanics.

However, the most unique feature of each faction is its exclusive set of Faction Laws — research options that change different aspects of the game. These Laws can: • Strengthen the economy • Improve creature stats • Modify core gameplay rules
As you play, you earn Law points, increasing your faction’s level. With each new level, you gain 1 Law Seal. These Seals can be spent immediately or saved to unlock more expensive and powerful Laws.
To unlock high‑level Laws, you must first invest some Seals into some basic ones.
Some Laws require multiple Seals to unlock or upgrade. Some Laws have multiple levels — each level increases the effect of the Law.

Law points are generated daily by cities and can also be earned by heroes during active play: • Defeating neutral squads grants Law points equal to 20% of the hero’s gained XP, excluding any additional bonuses. • Capturing neutral objects on the map also grants Law points.
Portals

While exploring the map, you may encounter portals — special objects that instantly transport heroes to another location. Unlike regular movement, where you always see the destination, portals can take you to unexplored and remote regions of the map.
Each portal is always connected to another portal.

Sometimes, access to a portal is guarded by a neutral squad. Defeat them, and your hero will be able to use the portal. You can return through the same portal without having to fight again — the guards only appear once.
Artifacts: Hero Items
While exploring the world, heroes can find and equip artifacts — powerful items that enhance their abilities or weaken opponents. Each hero can equip:

• 1 Main‑Hand Weapon and 1 Off‑Hand Weapon • 1 Head artifact, 1 Armor, 1 Belt, 1 pair of Boots, 1 Back artifact • 2 Rings • 4 Items • 1 Holy Relic — a special artifact that enhances the hero’s Skills.
Artifacts come in four rarities: common, rare, epic, and legendary. The higher the rarity, the more powerful the effect.
Many artifacts are part of sets. Equipping multiple items from the same set provides additional bonuses.
Artifacts from sets can be improved. This requires Alchemical Dust, and the upgrade is performed directly in the hero’s inventory.
Alchemical Dust
Alchemical Dust is a special resource required to improve various elements of the game. It is used to enhance artifacts, upgrade spells, and construct upgraded creature dwellings in cities.

Unlike other resources, there are no mines that produce Alchemical Dust. You can obtain it in the following ways: • Discovering it on the global map or by visiting certain objects • Synthesizing it in the Alchemical Lab using Crystals, Gems, and Mercury • Destroying artifacts to extract a small amount of dust.
Interacting with a Friendly Hero
In addition to moving around the map and visiting interactive objects, you can have your heroes approach one another.
When this happens, the exchange interface will open, allowing you to transfer artifacts and troops between heroes.

Distribute your forces wisely: split your army between heroes to defend different locations, or unite all your troops under one hero for decisive victories. Strategic interaction between your heroes is an essential skill that can help you overcome even the most formidable challenges.
Interacting with an Enemy Hero
In addition to moving around the map and visiting interactive objects, your heroes can encounter enemy heroes. When this happens, a battle will occur.
Opposing heroes engage in a special type of battle. Unlike fights against scattered neutral squads, the enemy can use spells, and their army is typically stronger and better organized.

At the end of the battle, one hero is killed, and the victorious hero receives experience as well as all the artifacts that belonged to the defeated hero. However, you or your opponent may flee before losing all their units, allowing the hero to be rehired (with their levels, skills, and artifacts intact) to fight another day.
City Siege
City Siege is a special type of battle that is triggered when a hero attempts to capture a city that is defended by at least one creature.
This type of battle differs from regular combat due to the presence of defensive structures on the defender’s side, especially if they have developed the city and built fortifications.
As an attacker, your goal is to destroy the defensive walls in order to reach the enemy creatures. A catapult can be used to destroy defensive structures.

As a defender, your task is to repel the enemy’s attack, either by forcing them to flee or by defeating them. Any defensive structures that you’ve built in that city, such as walls, towers, moats, and more, will aid you in your defense by directly altering the battlefield.
Battle Basics
Battles in Heroes of Might and Magic take place on separate battlefields, divided into hexes — hexagonal spaces. Creatures move across these hexes and engage in combat with enemies.
Battles proceed in rounds. Once all stacks, both yours and the enemy’s, have taken their turns, the next round begins.

The sequence of turns is determined by Initiative. The higher a creature’s Initiative value, the earlier their stack will act in each round. You can always check the turn order by looking at the initiative bar at the top of the screen.

Before the battle begins, the Tactical Phase is activated, during which you can adjust the placement of your troops. To do this, drag any friendly stack within the highlighted area. If you drag a stack onto a hex occupied by another stack, they will swap places.
Enemy Movement and Attack
The goal of the battle is to defeat every unit in the enemy army. To achieve this, your creatures must attack the enemy’s troops.
The game features creatures with three types of attacks:
Melee attack — The creature deals damage only when it is close to the target (i.e., on an adjacent hex).
Long-Reach attack — The creature can attack a target one hex directly behind adjacent hexes.
Ranged Attack — The creature can attack from any distance, unless there is an enemy adjacent to it. However, the damage decreases as the target moves further away.
If a stack can’t attack at the moment, it can move closer to the enemy. The maximum movement distance depends on the creature’s Speed.

A creature making a Melee or Long Reach attack can both approach and attack in the same turn. Move the mouse around the target hex to select which hex your creature will attack from.

Ranged attacks do not include an approach phase, so Ranged creatures must choose between moving or attacking.
Important: Any action — an attack, an ability, or a move — ends the creature’s turn, unless otherwise stated.
When your creatures attack an enemy in melee combat, they may receive a counterattack in response.
The number of counterattacks is limited — usually, creatures can counterattack only once per round.
In certain situations, if you don’t want a stack to attack or move, you can use additional tactical options:
• Wait — the stack delays its action until the end of the current round, allowing others to act first. This option can only be used once per round per stack.
• Skip Turn — the stack forfeits its action for the round and won’t be able to act until the next one.
Manage your troops wisely: plan their positioning on the battlefield and choose the optimal moment to strike to minimize losses from enemy counterattacks.
Creature Abilities

Many creatures have abilities — special active skills that can deal Damage, strengthen friendly creatures, weaken enemy creatures, and more. Unless stated otherwise, using an ability will end the creature’s turn.
To use abilities, units need Focus, which accumulates during the battle.
When a stack attacks or takes Damage from an enemy, it generates one Focus Point for its army. After accumulating 6 Focus Points, you get a Focus Charge, which can be spent to activate abilities.
Different abilities require different amounts of Focus Charges to activate. The required amount is shown on the ability icon.
After using the ability, it enters a cooldown and becomes unavailable for several rounds. The cooldown duration is equal to the ability’s activation cost.
Many creatures have powerful abilities that can significantly alter the course of a battle. Use Focus wisely!
Your hero can also use active abilities, which also require Focus Charges. These abilities follow the same rules as creature abilities — after use, they enter a cooldown.
You can use your hero’s active abilities at any point during your creatures’ turn. Using a hero’s ability does not end the creature’s turn.
All heroes start out with a simple ability that deals Damage to enemy targets. Other abilities are unlocked as you progress by learning special skills.
Use the Focus Charges your army accumulates wisely. Don’t forget to activate your creatures’ and heroes’ abilities at the right moment to maximize their effectiveness in battle.
Combat Magic
One way to win a battle is by casting spells that your hero knows.
During your creature’s turn, the hero can use their Spellbook to cast the desired spell.
There are four schools of magic, each containing a different type of effect. Heroes can cast up to one spell from each school per round.
Casting spells consumes mana, which does not automatically restore between battles, so it’s important to plan its usage carefully.
After you cast a spell in combat, it will become unavailable for several rounds. The higher its tier, the longer its cooldown.
During the game, you will be able to reduce the cooldown time of spells, but remember: Under no circumstances can you cast the same spell twice in a single round.
Defeating Guards
In addition to the usual battles on the global map, you may come across special guarded objects.

Battles with these guards differ from standard battles due to the unique placement of both your and your enemy’s troops. In these encounters, your forces may be surrounded by the enemy, or, in some cases, you may have the advantage of surrounding the enemy yourself.
Victory in such battles will reward you with the valuable prize that was being guarded by the creatures you just defeated.
Global Map Magic
Spells can be used not only in battle but also on the global map.
Global map spells are a special type of magic. These spells have a variety of impacts, allowing heroes to get access to hidden information, alter the rules of movement and interaction, and more.

In addition to consuming mana, global map spells have a daily usage limit — some of them can only be cast once or a few times per day. Once a new day begins, this counter resets, and the spells become available to cast again.

You can acquire global map spells in various ways: by discovering special artifacts on the map, learning specific hero skills, and more. However, the most powerful spells are unlocked through city development and studying magic in the Magic Observatory. As you construct buildings and upgrade spells within the Observatory, you will earn special points. Accumulate enough points, and you will unlock the most powerful global map spells.

You can acquire global map spells in various ways: by discovering special artifacts on the map, learning specific hero skills, and more. However, the most powerful spells are unlocked through learning them in the Magic Observatory. Your cities generate a small amount of Astrology points, which help you deepen your understanding of magic. By accumulating enough points, you will gain an Insight into the magic inscribed in the stars. You can spend it to learn or upgrade especially powerful global map spells.
Proper use of global map spells can significantly influence the course of the game, granting access to previously unreachable areas and objects.
Managing Multiple Heroes
In Heroes of Might and Magic, you can use multiple heroes to explore the map efficiently and quickly.
Each hero has their own level and set of skills, and they can each play various roles — from a simple scout and resource-collector to a great commander who can conquer your enemies.
Heroes can be hired in any city where a Tavern is built. Hiring heroes requires a one-time payment of Gold.

You can control only a limited number of heroes at a time. Capturing new cities increases this limit. However, if you lose control of cities, the maximum number of heroes decreases, and you may lose the ability to recruit new ones.
Your currently recruited heroes are displayed on the right side of the screen.
Creature Stats
Each creature in the game is a unique entity with its own stats as well as active and passive abilities.
Units of the same creature can be combined in a stack. The more units in a stack, the more Damage it deals. When taking Damage, units in the stack can die, which reduces the number of the stack.
In the creature information window, you can view the following stats:

• HP — the amount of health of one unit in the stack. • Attack and Defense — used in determining the Damage dealt by and to the creature. If your creature’s Attack is higher than the target’s Defense, the Damage increases; if lower, the Damage decreases. • Damage — the base Damage range of a single unit in a stack. • Morale and Luck — used in determining the probability of an additional turn or increased Damage during an attack. • Speed — determines the creature’s maximum range of movement in battle. • Initiative — determines the order of the creature’s turns in battle.
Creatures can also have active and passive abilities — special skills that can have a significant impact on the outcome of the battle.

Crystals: {0}/{1}
Mercury: {0}/{1}

Congratulations on successfully completing the mission!
In this mission, you must reach the marked location at the edge of the map. Manage your heroes’ Movement points carefully, and you’ll surely succeed!
In this mission, you must acquire 10 Crystals, 10 Gems, and 10 Mercury using the Marketplace and your hero’s abilities. For your convenience, your hero’s Movement points are unlimited.
In this mission, gather an army and defeat the enemy force in the lower part of the map within two turns. Note: a new game week will begin after you end your first turn.

In this mission, your hero has almost no army and must instead rely on combat magic. Empower your spells using every available method and defeat the enemy. For your convenience, your hero’s Movement points are unlimited, and battle spell cooldowns are reduced to a minimum.

In this mission, your hero has no remaining Movement points. Start a new day, flee or surrender from the battle with the enemy hero, and then return later with reinforcements and defeat them.
Pathfinding

Movement points must be managed with great care. As you already know, they are spent when heroes move, so using them wisely allows you to explore the map more efficiently and cover greater distances in a single turn.
The number of Movement points required for each step varies depending on the direction of movement; moving diagonally costs more points.

Terrain type also affects the cost of movement:
• Moving along roads reduces the cost.
• Moving through terrain increases the cost.
• Moving through river squares increases the cost even further.
However, there are times when a hero can move through difficult terrain without any penalty:
• If the terrain is native to the hero’s faction.
• If the terrain is native to the faction of all creatures in the hero’s army.
• If the hero has accumulated bonuses that reduce terrain penalties or can currently fly.
Plan your actions carefully on the global map to make sure your heroes and troops can get where they need to be!
Marketplaces and Resources
Throughout the game, you will need a variety of resources for constructing buildings, recruiting armies, upgrading spells, and more.

While many resources can be gathered naturally, sometimes you’ll face situations where a crucial resource isn’t readily available but is needed immediately. That’s when the Marketplace comes in handy.
The Marketplace is a special building that can be constructed in your cities or found on the global map. It allows you to exchange one type of resource for any other — except for Alchemical Dust.
When you first open the exchange interface, you might notice the exchange rates are unfavorable. Fortunately, you can improve these rates to make trades more beneficial.
To lower exchange rates: • Build as many Marketplaces as possible in your cities. • Learn hero skills that improve trade.
Be sure to build Marketplaces ahead of time, so you’re ready to exchange resources profitably when the need arises!
Weekly Growth
Your army is the key to victory. Winning battles won’t be easy without proper attention to your forces.
Creatures can be recruited both in your cities and at external dwellings scattered across the global map. Each week, new reinforcements become available for hire in these locations.

Most notably, external creature dwellings not only allow for direct recruitment but also passively increase the weekly growth of the corresponding creatures in all your cities. Your flag must be active at the beginning of the week for this growth bonus to take effect.
By managing your army effectively and controlling territories with these dwellings, you will steadily grow your forces and become powerful enough to confront your opponents.
Counterattack

Because your troops and resources are limited, minimizing your losses taken in each battle is vital. Sometimes the easiest way to do this is by attacking a clearly weaker unit who cannot offer proper resistance.
However, you will often face enemies who are equal to or stronger than your army, making the fight truly challenging.
In these situations, positioning your troops wisely on the battlefield is key — especially to avoid enemy counterattacks.

A common tactic is to use weaker, smaller, single‑unit stacks as bait. You can have them strike first, provoking the enemy’s counterattack, after which your main forces can safely act without risking further retaliation.

Distribute your forces thoughtfully — remember that your hero’s stacks can be divided into several smaller ones before you have engaged an enemy for battle. This flexibility can help you overcome even the most formidable foes.
Spell Levels
Victory in battle — and in the game as a whole — can be achieved not only through a powerful army, but also with the clever use of magic.
A hero who specializes in spellcasting can succeed where regular troops would struggle.
To make magic a truly formidable weapon, you’ll want to enhance your spells as much as possible.
Each spell has four levels of potency. Typically, a newly acquired spell starts at level one. As its level increases, the spell gains various improvements and becomes significantly more powerful.
If you’ve chosen to follow the path of magic, remember to upgrade your spells — as your enemies become stronger, so must you.
You can increase a spell’s level in several ways:
• Learn special hero skills and sub‑skills.
• Equip artifacts that boost spell levels.
• Use the Magic Observatory to manually add one level to a spell by spending Alchemical Dust. This upgrade is applied to all your heroes that learn that spell.
• Build Mage Guilds in your cities. If the same spell appears in two or more Guilds, it will automatically gain one level in the Observatory at no cost.
• Capture special map objects that raise spell levels in the Magic Observatory.
Areas and Map Spells

As you’ve learned, you can use global map spells to manipulate your heroes’ positioning and help you explore areas that might otherwise be unreachable. To learn those spells, you need to gain and spend an Astrological Insight.
However, in some games (if this limitation is enabled), you may find that certain places cannot be accessed via teleportation or flight. This is due to the map being divided into distinct areas.

Areas are special regions of the map, usually separated by specific terrain or boundaries. Each area contains its own set of resources, artifacts, and objects, and it may even affect some of your heroes’ passive abilities.
To view area borders, enable the corresponding overlay on the world map or press Z.
To unlock access to a new area, you typically need to defeat the neutral squad guarding its border. Until this army is defeated, that area remains inaccessible for global map spell use.
Note: You can only use global map spells on areas that currently have a walkable path that can be reached without triggering any nearby guards.

If you are unable to teleport or fly to a desired location, try locating a path to it on foot. Defeat the guardians blocking access, and the area will then allow the use of teleportation and similar spells.
Hero Chaining

Properly planned hero logistics on the global map are one of the most powerful tools for gaining a decisive advantage. Positioning your heroes with forethought allows you to explore efficiently and complete multiple objectives in a single turn.
Each hero can only reach a limited number of nearby objects. But what if you need to accomplish several tasks in one turn — or if your hero lacks the army to defeat a powerful enemy?

In such cases, you can perform a hero chain: a tactic of passing armies and artifacts from one hero to another. This allows you to move everything you need across great distances within the same turn.

Plan ahead by calculating how far each hero can travel and where they’ll end up the next day. Position them carefully so they’re ready to form a chain when needed. With well‑planned logistics, your strategic options will expand significantly, giving you a clear advantage on the map.
Flee and Surrender
Sometimes your hero may face a much stronger opponent. If the battle isn’t going in your favor, it’s not necessarily over. You always have two options: flee or surrender.
• Flee lets your hero leave the battle but abandons their entire army on the battlefield. Your hero keeps all artifacts and skills, and they can be rehired in one of your cities.
• Surrender allows your hero to safely leave the battle with their whole army — but you must pay the full value of your remaining creatures to the opponent.
You can choose to flee or surrender at any time during your creature’s turn during combat. Heroes that flee can be rehired from any of your cities, even far from the battle location.
Don’t hesitate to flee or surrender in critical moments — preserving your hero’s army, artifacts, and experience might let you come back stronger!
